hero.title: I build tools that make teams autonomous

about.title: Four years industrializing internal tools
about.p1: Apprentice at Groupe ESLC since September 2022, and freelancer in parallel. My playground: the internal tools that didn't exist, or that needed to be rebuilt properly.
about.p2: Concretely, I take a project from A to Z — architecture, front and back development, deployment, docs and follow-up. Solo if needed, in full autonomy.

stack.title: The tools I use day to day

hero.title: Degrés Jours — Automated weather pipeline
hero.pitch: ESLC manages heating across 20 sites with fuel contracts billed on monthly DJU data. I replaced several hours of monthly manual work with a fully automated pipeline: a C# script collects MétéoFrance data every day at 1:30 PM, injects it into MongoDB in under 10 seconds, and DJU are calculated and available in real time across all sites.

context.title: Several hours a month, one person, zero automation
context.p1: ESLC tracks heating across 20 sites via fuel contracts billed on monthly Unified Degree Days (DJU). Before MeteoESLC, this calculation was entirely manual: downloading MétéoFrance reports, copying them into Excel, calculating DJU column by column, emailing the file to each stakeholder, managing concurrent versions (v1, v2, v2_final…). This recurring process took several hours every month — and relied on a single person who knew the spreadsheet.
context.p2: The real consequence: a delay or error in monthly DJU could distort the fuel billing for all sites. The company's heating budget depended on a fragile Excel spreadsheet, maintained by hand, with no traceability and no centralization.

dju.title: From thermometer to energy contract in under 10 seconds
dju.explainerBody: A Unified Degree Day (DJU) measures how cold a day is: max(0, 18 − (TN + TX) / 2). When the daily average drops below 18 °C, buildings need heating — each degree below 18 is one DJU. Fuel suppliers use monthly cumulative DJU split into 3 decades (D1/D2/D3) to validate actual consumption and adjust billing.

steps.s1: 1:30 PM — SCR-01 queries MétéoFrance API for all 20 stations
steps.s3: Chokidar detects the file in under 5 seconds (SMB polling)
steps.s4: ingestService parses, idempotent upsert → MongoDB Atlas
steps.s5: All 4 business views updated — summary email at 1:35 PM

architecture.title: Parallel dual-write
architecture.leadIn: The C# script writes in parallel to two completely independent destinations. The ICopitole branch consumes .data files at 1:45 PM for energy management. The web portal branch goes through Chokidar → MongoDB → Next.js for real-time display. A failure on one branch never blocks the other.

timeline.title: The critical window: 10 minutes to fix before ICopitole
timeline.context: ICopitole consumes .data files at exactly 1:45 PM. If a station is in error, the operator has exactly 10 minutes — between the alert email at 1:35 PM and the point of no return at 1:45 PM — to fix and re-run.
timeline.clickHint: Click a step to see details

nominal.detail: The Windows scheduled task launches SCR-01. The script queries the MétéoFrance API for each of the 20 sites and collects yesterday's TN/TX data.

error.detail: The Windows scheduled task launches SCR-01. The script queries the MétéoFrance API — one or more stations return an error (timeout, missing data, API quota exceeded).

nominal.detail: SCR-01 completes its run. A summary email is sent automatically: 20/20 stations collected, .data files written to SRV21 and SRV22, MongoDB up to date.

error.detail: SCR-01 detects an anomaly and sends an alert email: failed stations listed, .data files partial or missing. The 10-minute intervention window starts now.

nominal.detail: Everything went smoothly. ICopitole will consume the files at 1:45 PM. Nothing to do.

error.detail: Last chance before the point of no return. An operator can correct missing data and manually re-trigger collection for failed stations via the admin portal.

nominal.detail: The ICopitole scheduled task runs. It reads the .data files present on SRV21 and SRV22 and integrates them into the energy management database. Pipeline complete.

error.detail: ICopitole consumes whatever is present in the .data files, even if incomplete. This is the point of no return: after this time, a correction requires manually re-running the downstream ICopitole tasks.

nominal.action: Data available on the web portal
nominal.detail: MongoDB data has been available on the web portal since 1:35 PM. The pipeline is complete and today's degree days are displayed for all 20 stations.

error.detail: If a correction was made after 1:45 PM, the downstream ICopitole tasks must be re-run manually to propagate the corrected data. The web portal can be updated independently via MongoDB.

decision1.title: usePolling: true — fs.watch doesn't work on SMB
decision1.content: Node.js native fs.watch never fires events on an SMB network share — a Windows kernel limitation, poorly documented. usePolling: true with interval: 5000 is the only reliable option. awaitWriteFinish.stabilityThreshold: 2000 prevents reading a partially written .data file. ignoreInitial: false re-ingests files already present on backend restart — essential on an unstable network.

decision2.title: Idempotent upsert — zero duplicates regardless of retry frequency
decision2.content: ingestService never uses insertOne — always updateOne on the (poste, date) filter with upsert: true. The unique index on (poste, date) enforces this at the database level. A manual re-run, watcher restart, or .data file rewrite never creates a duplicate. The operation is idempotent by design, not by convention.

decision3.title: POST /api/ingest fallback — the watcher is not a SPOF
decision3.content: If Chokidar goes down (SMB outage, Docker restart), the operator injects the file via POST /api/ingest from the admin portal. Same ingestService, same validation, same idempotent upsert. The watcher is not a single point of failure — the system continues in degraded mode without service interruption.

beforeAfter.title: What changed
beforeAfter.row1before: Several hours of manual data entry per month
beforeAfter.row2after: Under 10 seconds after the .data file is deposited
beforeAfter.row3before: Multiple versions in circulation (v1, v2, v2_final…)
beforeAfter.row3after: Single source of truth — MongoDB Atlas
beforeAfter.row5after: Available to everyone, from any workstation

pages.title: 4 business views, one source of truth
pages.intro: Each view addresses a specific operational need: the dashboard for daily monitoring, the daily report for historical lookup, the weekly trend for temperature patterns, and the monthly DJU report for fuel billing.

p1.title: Dashboard
p1.desc: Last imported day: KPIs (active stations, avg TN/TX, cumulated DJU), frost alerts, bar chart. Daily starting point.

p2.title: Daily report
p2.desc: Any past day, filterable by station. TN/TX bar chart and CSV export compatible with Excel (';' separator, UTF-8 BOM).

p3.title: Weekly trend
p3.desc: TN, Average and TX curves over an ISO week for a given station. Only weeks with data are shown.

p4.title: Monthly DJU report
p4.desc: D1/D2/D3 calculation over a full month. CSV export ready for fuel suppliers and energy contract tracking.

stations.title: 20 MétéoFrance stations, 20 ESLC sites
stations.description: For each ESLC site, the nearest active MétéoFrance station with available daily TN/TX data.

cta.confidentialText: For confidentiality and legal reasons, I cannot share access to this application. It is deployed in production on ESLC's internal network.

hero.title: FormRH — Automated onboarding & offboarding
hero.pitch: Before FormRH: 3 to 5 email exchanges per hire, manual Active Directory account creation with groups assigned from memory, and no tracking of post-creation tasks. Today: HR fills one form — the full workflow runs through to an active AD account. And all assignment rules (groups, OU, N2F) are editable from an admin interface without touching the code.

context.title: 3 to 5 emails, one manual creation, sometimes wrong permissions
context.p1: Every hire at ESLC triggered a manual chain: HR emailed the new employee's info to the manager, the manager replied with equipment needs, and IT manually created the Active Directory account — assigning groups from memory. Result: 3 to 5 emails per hire, wrong permissions on day one, and no tracking of post-creation tasks.
context.p2: FormRH automates the entire process, onboarding and offboarding. On the way in: an HR form triggers the full workflow through to AD account creation. On the way out: a dedicated page records the end date so IT can deactivate the account. And since the admin overhaul, all AD group assignment rules, OU paths, companies, and N2F configurations are editable from a web interface — no developer, no redeployment.

roles.title: From hire to active account — no manual email
roles.intro: FormRH structures the workflow around 4 roles. Each actor sees only what concerns them — HR doesn't see AD administration, managers only see their own employees.

s1.title: Employee record entry
s1.action: First name, last name, job type, department, city, start date, assigned manager
s1.auto1: Email automatically sent to the manager to complete the equipment form

s2.title: Validation + equipment needs
s2.action: Laptop/desktop, badge, phone, software (Icopitole, Sage, N2F…), free notes
s2.auto1: Daily automatic reminder email if the form isn't completed
s2.auto2: Summary email automatically sent to IT with all information upon validation

s3.title: AD account creation
s3.action: Select eligible employees + click "Create AD accounts"
s3.auto1: LDAP account created in the correct Organizational Unit (company + city)
s3.auto2: AD groups assigned according to job/department rules
s3.auto3: Credentials file (login + temporary password) generated and archived

s4.title: Post-creation task tracking
s4.action: Check off software and hardware installations in the /histo log
s4.auto1: Real-time dashboard: pending tasks, employees to process

s5.title: N2F account creation
s5.action: Triggered from the history record if N2F was checked in the manager form
s5.auto1: Name normalized (no accents), manager email fetched from Azure AD
s5.auto2: Account created via the N2F API with the appropriate analytics codes

architecture.title: Three layers, four integrations
architecture.leadIn: The Next.js frontend relies on a dedicated Express backend that orchestrates 4 independent integrations: Active Directory LDAP for account creation, Microsoft Graph API for authentication and manager lookup, the N2F API for expense accounts, and SMTP Exchange for automatic emails.

decision1.title: LDAP batch processing in groups of 3
decision1.content: The AD worker processes employees in batches of 3 rather than in bulk. Querying a domain controller with hundreds of simultaneous requests can saturate it and impact authentication for all network users. The batch-of-3 is a deliberate tradeoff: slightly slower, but with zero risk of production impact.

decision2.title: LRU cache for Microsoft Graph API
decision2.content: The Microsoft Graph API issues OAuth tokens with a 60-minute lifetime, but each renewal call costs 100-200 ms. With a 15-minute LRU cache, a valid token is reused for all requests within the window. The result: Graph API response time cut by 3× at peak activity.

decision3.title: Zero-downtime migration to MongoDB
decision3.content: The migration of configurations from static JSON files to MongoDB was done over two weeks of dual-write: every change was written to both systems in parallel, while the application still read from files. Once parity was verified, the cut-over switched the source of truth to MongoDB in a single config line — with no service interruption.

beforeAfter.title: What changed
beforeAfter.row2before: Manual AD creation, groups assigned from memory
beforeAfter.row4before: Post-creation tracked by email, no traceability
beforeAfter.row5after: 8 admin tabs, zero dev, zero redeployment

s1.title: Notification emails
s1.desc: At every workflow step, the right email goes out automatically — no need to remember to notify the manager or IT

s2.title: Daily reminders
s2.desc: If the manager hasn't completed the form, they receive a daily reminder email (cron job) until they do

s3.title: AD creation in 1 click
s3.desc: The worker creates the account in the correct LDAP OU, assigns AD groups based on job/department rules, and generates a credentials file

s4.title: N2F account
s4.desc: Automatic creation via the N2F API with name normalization and manager email fetched from Azure AD

s5.title: Config without redeployment
s5.desc: 8 admin tabs in MongoDB: new AD rules, new departments, new cities — without touching the code
